title: Anthropic Introduces 'Dreaming' to Let AI Agents Learn from Their Own Mistakes
summary: Anthropic unveiled 'dreaming' at its Code with Claude conference — a system that reviews AI agents' past sessions to extract patterns and improve their future performance without human intervention.
content: Anthropic unveiled 'dreaming' at its second annual Code with Claude developer conference in San Francisco, a capability that lets AI agents automatically review their past sessions, extract recurring patterns, and curate improvements for future runs — without modifying model weights. The system works alongside two other features now in public beta: outcomes, which uses a separate grader agent to evaluate work against a developer-defined rubric, and multi-agent orchestration, which splits complex tasks across specialist agents with independent context windows. Early results are striking: legal AI firm Harvey reported a 6x increase in task completion rates after implementing dreaming, while Wisedocs cut document review time by 50% using outcomes, and Netflix is now processing logs from hundreds of builds simultaneously using multi-agent orchestration. Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei also disclosed that the company saw 80x annualized revenue and usage growth in Q1 2026, far exceeding its internal projection of 10x annual growth, with API volume on the Claude platform up nearly 70x year over year.

title: OpenAI Launches GPT-Realtime-2 and Two New Voice API Models
summary: OpenAI released three new real-time audio models — GPT-Realtime-2, GPT-Realtime-Translate, and GPT-Realtime-Whisper — bringing GPT-5-class reasoning to live voice interactions and aggressive pricing that undercuts existing voice-AI vendors.
content: OpenAI released three new voice models in its API that bring GPT-5-class reasoning to live audio interactions. GPT-Realtime-2 handles audio in and out with reasoning inside the audio loop — eliminating the need for separate transcription, reasoning, and synthesis components — and features a 128K context window (up from 32K) that enables longer agentic voice conversations. GPT-Realtime-Translate supports live multilingual speech translation across 70+ input languages into 13 output languages, while GPT-Realtime-Whisper handles low-latency streaming transcription. On benchmarks, GPT-Realtime-2 scores 15.2% higher than its predecessor on Big Bench Audio; Zillow reported a 26-point lift in call-success rates on its hardest adversarial tests. Pricing is notably aggressive — GPT-Realtime-Translate at $0.034 per minute and GPT-Realtime-Whisper at $0.017 per minute significantly undercut most enterprise translation pipelines, putting pressure on standalone voice-AI vendors like ElevenLabs and Deepgram.

title: Microsoft, Google and xAI Agree to Let the Government Test Their AI Models Before Launch
summary: Google, Microsoft, and xAI struck pre-deployment testing agreements with NIST's Center for AI Standards and Innovation, joining Anthropic and OpenAI in a growing government oversight framework for frontier AI models.
content: Google, Microsoft, and xAI announced new partnerships with NIST's Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) to allow government evaluation of their frontier AI models before public launch, joining Anthropic and OpenAI in a growing pre-deployment testing framework. The move was triggered by Anthropic's disclosure of its Mythos model — which Anthropic says is 'far ahead' of competitors in cybersecurity capability — which sparked alarm among governments, banks, and utility companies and has been restricted to a small group of approved organizations. CAISI has already conducted over 40 model evaluations and will now test unreleased models from all five major AI labs for impacts on national security and public safety. The White House is separately weighing a formal executive-order-level review process for frontier AI, which would represent a departure from the Trump administration's largely hands-off approach to AI regulation. Experts note that CAISI is currently 'chronically underfunded' with approximately 30 staff and just $30 million in total funding since 2024, raising questions about its capacity to keep pace with rapid model development.

title: White House Studies AI Security Executive Order Modeled on FDA Drug Review Process
summary: The Trump administration is considering an executive order requiring frontier AI models to pass safety testing before public release — a potential 'FDA for AI' that would mark a significant shift in U.S. regulatory posture.
content: Kevin Hassett, director of the National Economic Council, said the Trump administration is studying a potential executive order that would require frontier AI models to be proven safe before public release, comparing the process to the FDA's drug approval framework. The proposal was prompted directly by Anthropic's disclosure of its Mythos model, which demonstrated the ability to rapidly find and exploit decades-old software vulnerabilities, alarming government agencies, banks, and utility companies. If issued, the order would likely centralize pre-deployment reviews at NIST's Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) — a significant expansion of responsibility for an agency experts describe as chronically underfunded, with just 30 staff and $30 million in total funding since its 2024 founding. The shift would mark a notable reversal for an administration that has touted a light-touch approach to AI, and officials were careful to note that 'any policy announcement will come directly from the President.' CAISI has already begun voluntary pre-deployment evaluations of models from Anthropic, OpenAI, Google DeepMind, Microsoft, and xAI.

title: Global Survey of 10,000 Businesses Finds AI Adoption at an Inflection Point
summary: A Dun & Bradstreet survey of 10,000 businesses across 32 countries found that 97% have active AI initiatives and 60% are now seeing measurable ROI, but only 5% say their data is truly ready to support AI at scale.
content: A new global survey by Dun & Bradstreet of 10,000 businesses across 32 countries found that AI adoption has reached an inflection point: 97% of organizations report active AI initiatives, 56% plan to increase AI investment in the next 12 months, and 60% now report at least some measurable ROI — a dramatic shift from the single-digit return rates that characterized 2025. Among those seeing results, 24% report broad or strong returns, 20% have multiple projects delivering ROI, and 30% are actively scaling AI into production. However, data readiness remains the critical bottleneck: only 5% of organizations say their data is fully ready to support AI at scale, with 50% citing limited data access, 40% reporting data quality concerns, and 37% pointing to a shortage of skilled AI professionals. The survey suggests the central constraint has shifted from model capability to the infrastructure and data foundations needed for AI agents to operate reliably across enterprise systems.

title: FDA Launches Elsa 4.0 and Consolidates 40+ Systems into New AI Data Platform HALO
summary: The FDA upgraded its internal AI tool to Elsa 4.0 and merged more than 40 disparate data portals into a unified platform called HALO, enabling staff to query regulatory submissions and build AI workflows without manual file uploads.
content: The U.S. Food and Drug Administration announced the launch of Elsa 4.0 — a major upgrade to its internal AI assistant available to all FDA staff — alongside the completion of HALO (Harmonized AI & Lifecycle Operations for Data), a platform that consolidates more than 40 disparate application and submission data systems across all FDA centers. Previously, staff had to manually upload documents into Elsa; now Elsa sits on top of HALO's unified data layer, allowing reviewers to query submissions and build automated workflows directly within the AI interface. New Elsa 4.0 capabilities include custom agents, document generation, quantitative data analysis with chart creation, voice-to-text dictation, OCR for scanned documents, and secure web search. The system runs in a FedRAMP High Google Cloud environment and does not train on any regulated-industry data. FDA Commissioner Marty Makary said the upgrades are designed to let agency scientists 'focus more on science' and accelerate the path from regulatory review to patient treatments.
